tasks.task_name: Find warm backpacking destination (1-2 night, mid-March)
tasks.first_domino: Open Google Maps and search warm hiking areas within 3-hour flight
tasks.hook_line: Can you find a warm backpacking spot that's actually warm in mid-March and won't break the budget?
tasks.reward: Locks in the adventure part of the trip — flights and gear planning can't happen until you pick the place
nuggets: Big Bend TX peaks at 75°F mid-March
Red Rock Canyon NV open year-round, 70s
Joshua Tree CA permits free, 80°F forecast
tasks.system_prompt: Help me find a warm backpacking destination for March 14-15, 2026 (this weekend). I'm flying from San Francisco. Need somewhere that's actually warm (70°F+), has 1-2 night backpacking trails, and is within a 3-hour flight. After this I'm flying to Chicago on March 16th for my friend's 30th birthday. I want trail recommendations with permit requirements, difficulty level, and flight options from SFO. Focus on Southwest US or anywhere warm and accessible.

tasks.task_name: Figure out REI gear rentals (backpack, tent, sleeping bag)
tasks.first_domino: Go to REI website and search for backpacking gear rental packages
tasks.hook_line: First time renting gear from REI — they do full backpacking kits and you can pick them up same-day.
tasks.reward: Solves the gear problem without buying — lets you test backpacking before committing to expensive equipment
nuggets: REI rents complete backpacking packages
Pick up at local store, return after trip
Tent + pack + bag usually $80-120 for weekend
tasks.system_prompt: Help me understand REI gear rental options for a 1-2 night backpacking trip March 14-15. I don't own backpacking gear yet. I need a backpack, tent (1-2 person), and sleeping bag suitable for warm weather (70°F+ days, 50°F nights). I'm in San Francisco — tell me what REI rents, typical pricing, how reservation and pickup works, and what I'd still need to bring myself. Also tell me if buying budget gear makes more sense if I'm planning to do this more often.

tasks.first_domino: Open Southwest and search SFO to destination for March 14 morning
tasks.hook_line: 6 days out and flights are still wide open — prices will jump if you wait past today.
tasks.reward: Backpacking trip is real — no more hypothetical, you're locked in
nuggets: Southwest usually cheapest to Southwest US
Friday morning flights less crowded
Book now, prices rise within 7 days
tasks.system_prompt: I need to book flights from SFO to [destination] for March 14, 2026 (departing morning) and returning March 15 (late afternoon or evening). Then I need a connecting flight from [destination] to Chicago arriving March 16. Help me find the best flight options on Southwest or budget airlines, with timing that gives me full days for backpacking March 14-15. Total budget ideally under $400 for both legs.

tasks.task_name: Book flight from backpacking to Chicago
tasks.first_domino: Open Southwest and search destination to Chicago for March 16
tasks.hook_line: Chicago flights already booked, this is the last flight puzzle piece to connect the whole trip.
tasks.reward: Completes the travel logistics — after this it's just packing and showing up
nuggets: Most warm Southwest airports connect to Chicago
March 16 arrival gives you evening with Mariano
Southwest allows free cancellation if plans change
tasks.system_prompt: I need to book a flight from [backpacking destination] to Chicago (ORD or MDW) on March 16, 2026. Should arrive by early evening so I can meet up with friends. This is the connecting leg after a backpacking trip March 14-15. Help me find Southwest or budget airline options that maximize time outdoors on the 15th but still get me to Chicago with time to settle in. Budget ~$200 for this leg.

tasks.first_domino: Text Mariano and ask what he's into lately or wants for his 30th
tasks.hook_line: What do you get a guy turning 30 who you're already giving a whole backpacking trip?
tasks.reward: Shows up with something thoughtful instead of scrambling at the airport or showing up empty-handed
nuggets: Experience gifts often better than objects for 30th
Chicago has deep-dish pizza gift certificates
Outdoor gear stores have gift cards if he's into hiking
tasks.system_prompt: Help me brainstorm a 30th birthday gift for my friend Mariano. I'm already organizing a backpacking trip with him March 14-15 and flying to Chicago for his birthday celebration March 19th. Budget $50-100. He's into outdoor activities (hence the backpacking) and lives in Chicago. I want something thoughtful but not over-the-top since the trip itself is the main gift. Give me 5-7 specific gift ideas with where to buy them.

tasks.task_name: Look up eyeglasses stores in Kyoto for early trip shopping
tasks.first_domino: Google 'best eyeglasses stores Kyoto English-friendly' and open Google Maps
tasks.hook_line: Kyoto's optical shops are hidden gems — locals swear by them for quality frames at half US prices.
tasks.reward: Gets the glasses shopping done early in the trip so you can stop thinking about it and enjoy temples
nuggets: Kyoto Station area has multiple optical shops
JINS and Zoff chains have English menus
Frames start ¥5000, lenses add ¥3000-8000
tasks.system_prompt: Help me find eyeglasses stores in Kyoto that are foreigner-friendly and can make glasses while I wait or within 1-2 days. I'm arriving March 25, 2026 and want to buy 4 pairs during my 2.5 days there (March 25-27). I have a US prescription and need to know: which stores speak English, typical pricing for frames + lenses, how long it takes to get glasses made, and where they're located relative to central Kyoto hotels. Also tell me what documentation I need to bring.

tasks.task_name: Validate insurance/prescription requirements for buying 4 pairs of glasses
tasks.first_domino: Email insurance provider asking about international eyeglasses coverage and reimbursement
tasks.hook_line: 4 pairs in Japan could save $400+ vs US prices — but only if insurance plays nice with international receipts.
tasks.reward: Clears the uncertainty — you'll know exactly what receipts to save and whether buying 4 pairs makes financial sense
nuggets: Most vision insurance covers international purchases with receipts
VSP typically reimburses out-of-network at 70%
Need itemized receipt in English showing frame/lens costs
tasks.system_prompt: Help me figure out how to get vision insurance reimbursement for eyeglasses purchased in Japan. I have VSP vision insurance and plan to buy 4 pairs of eyeglasses in Kyoto/Tokyo March 25-April 5, 2026. I need to know: Does VSP cover international eyeglass purchases? What documentation do I need to collect (receipts, prescriptions, etc.)? Can I use my US prescription or do I need to get an eye exam in Japan? Are there limits on how many pairs I can get reimbursed for in one year? Give me a step-by-step plan for maximizing coverage.

tasks.first_domino: Open Google Maps and search Kyoto temples worth visiting early morning
tasks.hook_line: Kyoto has 2000+ temples — which ones are actually worth the early wake-up and which are tourist traps?
tasks.reward: Closes out Kyoto planning — only Takayama and Tokyo left on the itinerary
nuggets: Fushimi Inari free entry, open 24hrs, best before 8am
Arashiyama bamboo grove magical before 9am crowds
Kinkaku-ji ¥500 entry, opens 9am, photograph peak 10-11am
tasks.system_prompt: Help me plan activities for 2.5 days in Kyoto (March 25-27, 2026). Traveling with my girlfriend Dana. We like temples, gardens, walking, quiet outdoor spaces, and good food. Already have hotels booked in central Kyoto. I want a day-by-day itinerary with opening hours, entry fees, Google Maps links, and a suggested walking route. Include a mix of major landmarks (Fushimi Inari, Golden Pavilion, Arashiyama) and less touristy spots. We're early risers and want to beat crowds. Also need to fit in eyeglasses shopping (see previous task).

tasks.first_domino: Google 'Takayama hiking trails day trips English' and check weather forecast
tasks.hook_line: Takayama is the Japanese Alps in early spring — alpine villages, mountain trails, and way fewer tourists than Kyoto.
tasks.reward: Balances the trip with outdoor adventure after all the city walking and temple viewing
nuggets: Old Town Takayama walkable in 3 hours
Shinhotaka Ropeway gives Alps views without hiking
Kamikochi opens April 15, too early for your dates
tasks.system_prompt: Help me plan activities for 2 days in Takayama (March 29-30, 2026). Traveling with my girlfriend Dana. We want a break from city crowds after Kyoto — looking for mountain scenery, hiking or nature walks, and traditional Alpine village vibes. We're staying in Takayama proper. Give me day-by-day options including Old Town highlights, nearby day trips (ropeway, valleys, trails), realistic travel times, and what's actually open/accessible in late March. Also flag what to skip if weather is bad.

tasks.first_domino: Google 'best Tokyo neighborhoods for first-time visitors' and open Google Maps
tasks.hook_line: Tokyo is 13 million people packed into neighborhoods that each feel like their own city — which ones are worth your limited time?
tasks.reward: Finishes the Japan trip planning — you'll have the full itinerary ready with 2 weeks to spare
nuggets: Shibuya/Harajuku for youth culture, 1 day max
Asakusa temple + old Tokyo, morning-only crowds
Tsukiji Outer Market best breakfast, arrive 8am
tasks.system_prompt: Help me plan activities for 6 days in Tokyo (March 31 - April 5, 2026). Traveling with my girlfriend Dana. We like food, interesting neighborhoods, some shopping (fountain pens, watches, tech), temples/gardens as palate cleansers, and people-watching. Already exhausted from 2 weeks of travel at this point. I want a flexible day-by-day framework organized by neighborhood, with must-sees, optional adds, food recommendations, and realistic pacing. Include fountain pen shops (Sailor limited editions), watch stores (Grand Seiko, used market), and eyeglasses stores if we didn't finish in Kyoto. Build in downtime.

tasks.first_domino: Open VS Code workspace and check TODO.md for next feature to implement
tasks.hook_line: Can you build an extension that makes Cursor actually respect your task context instead of wandering off?
tasks.reward: Gets you one step closer to a tool that solves your actual workflow problem with AI coding
nuggets: VS Code extension API docs are surprisingly good
Cursor uses same extension model as VS Code
Webview panels can show live task visualization
tasks.system_prompt: Help me continue building a VS Code extension called Task Pilot. Goal: Help developers maintain task focus during AI-assisted coding sessions. The extension should visualize work in progress and provide AI (like Cursor) with context about the current task while editing a shared document locally. I want to avoid the Craft agent system which doesn't integrate well with Cursor projects. Where should I focus next: task state management, AI context injection, UI visualization, or file watching? Give me specific implementation advice for the most impactful next feature.

tasks.first_domino: Sketch 3 UI layouts on paper showing task tree, progress, and context
tasks.hook_line: Most dev tools show you the code — this one shows you the thought process behind the code.
tasks.reward: Defines what the extension actually looks like before you waste time building the wrong UI
nuggets: VS Code webview panels support React or vanilla JS
Tree view in sidebar matches VS Code patterns
Status bar items give quick glanceable state
tasks.system_prompt: Help me design the UI for a VS Code extension that helps developers maintain task focus. I need to show: (1) current task and subtasks in a tree, (2) task progress and completion state, (3) relevant context (files, notes, blockers), (4) what the AI knows about the current task. The UI should be ADHD-friendly — low noise, high signal, easy to update without context switching. Should this be a sidebar tree view, a webview panel, status bar items, or a combination? Give me 3 concrete layout options with pros/cons.

tasks.first_domino: Research VS Code extension API for context injection into AI assistants
tasks.hook_line: How do you programmatically tell Cursor what you're working on without manually pasting context every time?
tasks.reward: This is the core value prop — automatic context without manual labor means AI actually stays on task
nuggets: Cursor reads .cursorrules for project context
VS Code extension can watch files and update context
Markdown format keeps AI context human-readable
tasks.system_prompt: Help me build a context management system for a VS Code extension that feeds task information to AI coding assistants like Cursor. I need to: (1) capture current task state (title, subtasks, blockers, relevant files), (2) format it in a way AI can consume, (3) inject it into Cursor's context automatically when I start a coding session. How does Cursor read context? Can VS Code extensions write to .cursorrules dynamically? What's the best way to keep context synced as tasks change? Give me an implementation strategy with code examples.

recommended_slots.reason: 7 days out, weekend energy perfect for trip research, medium effort matches ramp_up band
Low effort research pairs well with slot 1, batch-able with other Japan tasks, 16 days is comfortable but should start soon
Quick win under 30 min, low context cost, batches with slot 1 research session, unblocks gear planning
